elements.sent-2411-009: The set of all functions from a set $X$ into itself, denoted as $T(X)$, forms a regular semigroup under the composition of functions.
elements.sent-2411-010: This semigroup is known as the full transformation semigroup on X and is important in algebraic semigroup theory.
elements.sent-2411-011: Similar to Cayley's Theorem for groups, it can be shown that any semigroup $S$ can be embedded in the full transformation semigroup $T(S^1)$ where $S^1$ is a monoid obtained from $S$ by adjoining an identity if necessary.

elements.sent-2411-013: Pei \cite{Pei introduced a new semigroup called the transformation semigroup that preserves equivalence, $T_E(X)$, defined by \begin{equation}T_{E}(X)=\{\alpha\in T(X):\forall x,y\in X,(x,y)\in E\Rightarrow (x\alpha,y\alpha)\in E\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This semigroup is a generalization of the full transformation semigroup $T(X)$ and is defined as a subsemigroup of $T(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Clearly, if $E=X\times X$, then $T_E(X)$ is equal to $T(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the field of topology, $T_E(X)$ refers to the semigroup comprising all continuous self-maps of a given topological space $X$ that satisfy the condition of having the $E$-classes serve as a basis.
elements.sent-2411-017: This semigroup is commonly denoted as $S(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-018: The regularity of elements and Green's relations for $T_E(X)$ were investigated in \cite{Pei .. In 2008, L.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Cheng \cite{Sun characterized the natural partial order $\leq$ on $T_E(X)$ and studied its compatibility.}
elements.sent-2411-021: They also described the maximal, minimal, and covering elements of $T_E(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Xu \cite{Deng introduced a new semigroup called the transformation semigroup that preserve double direction equivalence, $T_{E^* (X)$, defined by \begin{equation}T_{E^*}(X)=\{\alpha\in T(X):\forall x,y\in X,(x,y)\in E\Leftrightarrow (x\alpha,y\alpha)\in E\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: We can see that this semigroup is a subsemigroup of the transformation semigroup that preserves equivalence, $T_E(X)$, and it is defined as the set of all functions in $T_E(X)$ that preserve the reverse direction of the equivalence relation $E$ on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-026: If $E$ is the universal relation on $X$, then $T_{E^* (X)$ is equal to $T(X)$, which means that this semigroup is also a generalization of $T(X)$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Additionally, $T_{E^* (X)$ is a semigroup consisting of continuous self-maps of the topological space $X$ where the $E$-classes form a basis.}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is commonly referred to as a semigroup of continuous functions (see \cite{Magill for details).}

elements.sent-2411-032: They also described the maximal and minimal elements and studied the existence of the greatest lower bound of two elements in this semigroup.

elements.sent-2411-036: If the set $X$ is partitioned by the equivalence relation $E$ into subsets $A_i$ for all $i$ in the index set $I$, the author defined the subsemigroup $Q(\mathbf{2 )$ of $\reg(T)$ as follows: \begin{equation}Q(2)=\{\alpha\in \reg(T):|A_i\alpha|<2\ for all\ i\in I\},\end{equation} or, equivalently, \begin{equation}Q(2)=\{\alpha\in T_{E^*}(X):|A_i\alpha|=1\ and\ A_i\cap X\alpha\neq\emptyset\ for all\ i\in I\}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-195: Let's revisit the concept of a maximal subsemigroup of a semigroup $S$.
elements.sent-2411-196: A maximal subsemigroup of $S$ refers to a subset of $S$ that is both a proper subsemigroup (i.e., not equal to $S$) and not contained within any other proper subsemigroup of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-197: Analogously, a maximal proper subgroup of a group $G$ can be defined as a subgroup that cannot be contained within any other proper subgroup of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-198: It is well-known that, when $G$ is a finite group, every subsemigroup of $G$ becomes a subgroup.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this section, we will provide a description and enumeration of the maximal subsemigroups present in any right group which can be written as the direct product of a finite group and a right zero semigroup, as well as in $Q_{E^* (X)$, where $X$ is a finite set.}
elements.sent-2411-200: We begin this section by stating the following lemma.

elements.sent-2411-204: It follows that $H$ is a subgroup of $G$ since $G$ is finite.
elements.sent-2411-205: Moreover, $F$ is also a right zero semigroup.
elements.sent-2411-206: Therefore, $T$ can be written as the direct product of the group $H$ and the right zero semigroup $F$, and thus it is also a right group.
